content:
For type inference of functions that might be custom type methods, it seems like we've got ambiguity.

module [U64Pair generating [to_str]]

U64Pair := { left: U64, right: U64 }

add_up : U64Pair -> U64
add_up = |pair| pair.left + pair.right

double_left = |item| item.left * 2

I'd assume the type of double_left is { left: Num a, .. } -> Num a. Since we don't have an active destructing syntax for custom values, it seems like we would need to say U64Pair has the following methods:

  • Any method generated by the compiler (to_str in this case)
  • Any function that has U64Pair as the type of the first arg
  • Any function whose first type is compatible with the inner type of U64Pair
    • Since { left: Num a, .. } is a supertype of { left: U64, right: U64 }, double_left is included

Or is that last case not allowed? In which case, we may want to consider one of the following changes:

  • prefixing methods with the type name, e.g. U64Pair.double_left = |item| item.left * 2
  • Having an explicit destructuring like double_left = |item.{ left, .. }| left * 2
does @U64Pair not work to destructure?
That's a third option yes
I'd be suprised if that's desired, however, as the latest suggestion seems to be that we would try to find a version of Roc that doesn't need to use @U64Pair

Sam Mohr said:

module [U64Pair generating [to_str]]

U64Pair := { left: U64, right: U64 }

add_up : U64Pair -> U64
add_up = |pair| pair.left + pair.right

double_left = |item| item.left * 2

I'd assume the type of double_left is { left: Num a, .. } -> Num a.

in the proposal, a * b would desugar to a.times(b), so the type of double_left would be: (edited because I forgot it would be inferred to be an open record)

{ left : a, ..b } -> c
    where a.times(Num d) -> c
And this function would be definable in the U64Pair module, but would not be definable in any other module, right? At least not in a way that supports U64Pair (cause ti doesn't expose it's fields)

if you implemented it as this:

double_left = |item| Num.times(item.left)

...then the type of double_left would indeed be:

double_left { left : Num a, .. } -> Num a

but I don't think there's any ambiguity here. If you call my_u64_pair.double_left() it's just straightforwardly a type mismatch:

  • static dispatch says the way we evaluate this is to first infer the type of my_u64_pair, which let's say turns out to be a U64Pair
  • then it looks up the module where U64Pair is defined
  • then it looks for a top-level function in that module named double_left, which it finds
  • then it calls that function passing my_u64_pair, which is a type mismatch because that function takes a Num a, not a U64Pair
this was one of my fears about using the name "methods" to refer to these: they feel more formal than they actually are :sweat_smile:

the foundational idea of the static dispatch proposal is that:

  • there is only one type of function in the language: a function. There is no other category of function that exists. Methods aren't a thing. There are just functions.
  • there is a syntax which looks up where a function's implementation can be found using its type instead of using scope
  • that's it, that's the whole idea
so the only way ambiguity could be possible is if we had a syntactic ambiguity, which is the reasoning for keeping a strict separation between .foo and .foo()

I think this is more a nominal type question than a static dispatch question.

In some other module if I write U64Pair.double_left(some_u64_pair) is that valid? Assuming the module above explicitly exposing double_left without any type annotation.
Cause if I were to define the same function in another module, it definitely would fail
Cause U64Pair does not expose it's internals
yeah exposing rules don't change; if you can't access the function from where you're trying to call it (whether via static dispatch or direct call) you get an error
literally all the dispatch does is answer the question "where is this function's implementation?"
You can access the function, but it is unclear if the function is on the nominal type or a structural record.
it doesn't change any rules about what calling it means, exposure access, etc. etc.

Brendan Hansknecht said:

You can access the function, but it is unclear if the function is on the nominal type or a structural record.

hm, I don't quite follow - U64Pair.double_left(some_u64_pair) isn't a static dispatch call, it's just a normal direct function call
so either the type of some_u64_pair matches the type expected by U64Pair.double_left's first argument, or it doesn't; static dispatch isn't in any way involved in that expression

Modue exposing nominal type but not the internals of the nominal type.

module [U64Pair, double_left, typed_double_right, new]

U64Pair := { left: U64, right: U64 }

new : {left: U64, right: U64} -> U64Pair
new = {left, right} -> U64Pair.{left, right}

typed_double_right : U64Pair -> U64
typed_double_right = |item| item.right * 2

double_left = |item| item.left * 2

Some other module:

Import U64Pair

local_double_left = |item| item.left * 2

main = |_|
    x = { left: 6, right: 8 }
    # Definitely valid
    _ = local_double_left x
    _ = U64Pair.double_left x

    # Definitely invalid
    _ = U64Pair.typed_double_right x

    # Again definitely valid
    pair = U64Pair.new x
    _ = U64Pair.typed_double_right pair

    # Definitely invalid
    _ = local_double_left pair

    # unsure
    _ = U64Pair.double_left pair
Okay, we could move this to the #ideas > custom types topic, but Brendan is on what's really the problem
The current syntax for destructuring custom types doesn't make it clear that a custom type is being used without an annotation
So literally all custom type methods need to be annotated or else none of the "methods" will have the custom type as the inferred type, but the inner type
ah, the # unsure one should definitely be a compile-time error
one way to think of it is that, from a types perspective, calling a function that takes an open record has the same type unification happening as trying to access the record directly
(or trying to use a pattern match to destructure it)
so since U64Pair.double_left takes an open record, regardless of what module the function happens to be defined in, I still can't call it from a module that can't access the custom type's fields
moving double_left into the current module wouldn't change anything
another way to think of it: the current module doesn't "know" that there is a field on U64Pair named left (because its fields aren't exposed)
so if you have a value of type U64Pair and you try to pass it to a function that expects a record with a field named left, then of course that's a type mismatch
because as far as the current module knows, that value does not have that field
This is definitely a significant change from current roc. You now require a type signature on a function to expose it correctly. Roc currently never requires type signatures on functions.
oh interesting
yeah this would be an example of "you delete the type annotation and the code no longer compiles"
exactly
hm, that's very annoying
Okay, good to know I'm not crazy
yeah I don't think it's ambiguous, but it is unambiguously a problem :laughing:
a straightforward fix would be to say that custom records can't expose their fields
so they can be accessed where they're defined, but other modules never can
at which point they work more like today's opaque types
It is a really nice property that you can just do Ok if Result.Ok is imported, that would break, right?
at which point we could also do what Rust does with custom tag unions and say they always expose all of their tags
since if you want something to be opaque, you can just use a custom record to wrap it
So how would I write double_left without a signature and get it to infer as a U64Pair function in this scenario?
That's my question here
47 messages were moved here from #ideas > static dispatch - proposal by Sam Mohr.

Sam Mohr said:

So how would I write double_left without a signature and get it to infer as a U64Pair function in this scenario?

I don't think it's possible in the current design
obviously with opaque types it's straightforward
Okay, sure

oh, actually - you could change the implementation to use a qualified destructure:

double_left = |item|
    U64Pair.{ left } = item
    left * 2
so it is possible, it just looks different
It's a mirror of the construction syntax, so I'm in
Isn't this just opaque types then?
yeah, except that you don't have to use that syntax
within the module, you can use fields normally, which would be more convenient than today
You just have to type every function or use the syntax?
like today you have to destructure with @U64Pair before you can access any fields
hm
Seems like we've lost principle type inference?
Still means you are losing the ability to delete any type annotation and the code will still compile.
yeah, I guess even if we make them private-only it doesn't fix that
Alternatively, U64Pair.double_left = |{ left }| left * 2 solves this.
right, but it's still possible to choose to write it the other way
at which point if you delete the annotation you can get compile errors
which is not something that should happen
really unfortunate, guess the custom types design needs more iteration :big_smile:
great catch!
Kept me up thinking last night!
actually it can still work if custom tag unions can be either public or private, but custom records can only be public
because private tags have to be fully qualified by type
unlike field access

another way to think of it: Elm and Haskell have the equivalent of "custom tag unions can be either public or private" (and that's how they provide opaque types), so if you have that system but no concept of custom records, then clearly you still have principal decidable type inference
and then if you add custom records onto that, but all their fields are always public, then presumably that doesn't break anything
So you wrap a record in a custom tag to make it private?
yeah, same as in Elm or Haskell
at which point it would probably make more sense to just wrap a structural record
and custom records are really just for static dispatch on what would otherwise be structural records?
yeah
Yeah, this sounds good
I guess it maybe promotes more public nominal types via custom records, but maybe that is fine.
it certainly makes them more convenient than opaque types haha
but if the alternative is to add another language primitive (e.g. reintroducing a separate concept of opaque types like today, in addition to nominal types) it seems better to have fewer primitives
You could only have nominal tags
With the option to be public or private
true, that's how Elm does it
Not really sure the tradeoffs here though
So I don't think I have much of a comment or preference past, public/private custom tags feels like opaque types with a bit more flexibility
yeah it's definitely more flexibility!

this would make it more verbose to use opaque types than today though, e.g.

Email := [Email Str]

to_str : Email -> Str
to_str = |Email.Email(str)|
    str

or something like:

Email := [V Str]

to_str : Email -> Str
to_str = |Email.V(str)|
    str

maybe the "1-tuple" idea could be reconsidered as a "custom wrapper" type, e.g.

Email := Str

to_str : Email -> Str
to_str = |Email.(str)|
    str

from_str : Str -> Email
from_str = |str|
    Email.(str)
so it's like Foo := [ ... ] means custom tag union, and then separately Foo := ... means private wrapper

or maybe could be more explicit about when you want it to be a wrapper, e.g. something like:

Email := .(Str)

to_str : Email -> Str
to_str = |Email.(str)|
    str

from_str : Str -> Email
from_str = |str|
    Email.(str)

so .(Str) meaning "I want to do the .(str) thing on this to destructure/create it"
I guess that design is essentially equivalent to our current opaque types design, except that it would have syntax based on custom type syntax rather than the @
So maybe we want opaque types and then separately nominal tags and records (where the internals are public)?
yeah
but kinda unify the syntax
and I guess at that point we might as well just keep the name "opaque type"
What should the next step be here? Seems like we're letting the idea settle before we make any decisions. Is there something I can do to help sort out what this will look like?
yeah I'd like to leave it for a few days, see if anyone thinks of any other things to bring up, etc.

I think if you want a function argument type to be a custom type, you should have to explicitly specify it in the function declaration.

# The type stays the same with or without the annotation
double_left : U64Pair -> U64
double_left = |U64Pair.item| # insert actual syntax here
    item.left * 2

If you omit the explicit identification of a custom type, then you get a structural record instead.

double_left : {left: U64}a -> U64 # plus the static dispatch part of the type for times
double_left = |item|
    item.left * 2

If you call a function that accepts a structural record with a custom type and the fields required by the function are exposed, then all is well. If they are not exposed, then it is a type error.

double_left : {left: U64}a -> U64
double_left = |item|
    item.left * 2

x = double_left(U64Pair.new { left: 1, right: 1}) # Ok if `U64Pair.left` is exposed, error if it is not

If you call a function that accepts a custom type with a structural record, that is a type error.

double_left : U64Pair -> U64
double_left = |U64Pair.item| # insert actual syntax here
    item.left * 2

x = double_left({left: 1}) # type error

This maintains the property that adding or removing a type annotation won't change if your code compiles and it doesn't require the addition of a notion of opaque types in addition to custom types. It feels natural to me also. Thoughts?

I agree that you should have to explicitly specify the custom type as a prefix to the value. We need it for principle type inference. I think we'd have a really simple and complete systems if we supported this for records, tags, and tuples (which support aliases via 1-tuples).

CustomRecord := { field: Str }

use_record = |CustomRecord.{ field }| # or CustomRecord.data

CustomTag ok err := [Ok ok, Err err]

use_tag = |tag_data|
    when tag_data is
        CustomTag.Ok ok -> ...
        CustomTag.Err err -> ...

CustomTuple a := (U64, U64, a)

use_tuple = |CustomTuple.(first, second, third)|
    first + second + third

CustomSet item := (Dict item {},)

use_set = |CustomSet.(inner_dict)|
    inner_dict.method()

It's consistent, it requires no type annotations, and it works with dot syntax well. I don't think there's been a need for public opaque records or tuples, and this still supports public tags
To make this explicit, I think we should disallow just having CustomAlias := Str which desugars to a 1-tuple, I think it's 3 extra characters to make it a 1-tuple and leads to less confusion
Rust has found success with new-type single field tuple wrappers, I think it's better to be explicit, and it's simpler overall

what’s the issue with wrapping/unwrapping in the same way opaque types work today? if you create local getter like field = |@Opaque({field})| field then you can still access fields without unwrapping.
TBH I think you're right and we've basically just circled back around to opaque types, at least syntactically
I think the original discussion was that we wanted to make a simple way to define concrete types that can be passed over FFI worry-free
And opaque types were heavier than structural ones
So can we come up with something lighter-weight?
And the answer seems to be no within the defining module
Though I definitely think we want the change of exporting tags from a custom/opaque union, that makes results much nicer to work with from a static dispatch perspective
why are opaque types heavier than structural ones over ffi? they currently compile to the same representation, is that changing?
More in terms of syntactically instantiating them than their representation at runtime, I believe
You're right that they're all just aliases
I believe that's the value of public custom records, a.k.a. records from the top of a module

module [HttpRequest.*]

HttpRequest := { method: Method, url: Str, body: List U8 }

Sam Mohr said:

Though I definitely think we want the change of exporting tags from a custom/opaque union, that makes results much nicer to work with from a static dispatch perspective

one option for this is

Opaque := [A, B]
A = @Opaque A

which seems okay to me
The fact that you can freely pass custom records into functions accepting structural records is a big difference between today's opaque types and the proposed custom types
i see
Well, how do you make Result.Ok and Result.Err look how they do now, but work with static dispatch?
what if you made Ok and Err functions?
I feel like a bad spokesperson for custom types, there's been a lot of discussion after the proposal and I have no idea how close my mental state is to the current plan
That sounds like a similar approach to what we do for Bool.true and Bool.false
The problem is that we can't match on booleans outside of Bool.roc
i don't think there's a reason you can't do that though (implementation wise)
We'd no longer be able to match on Results ourselves, we'd only be able to use Result methods
Wouldn't that break encapsulation to allow exhaustive matching of an opaque (read: private) tag from outside its defining module?
for opaque types yes, but my understanding is that custom types' internals are visible? although i know there's some talk about private fields too
I'm gonna go buy groceries and reread the proposal while I'm out, I'm not sure what's real anymore haha

Isaac Van Doren said:

The fact that you can freely pass custom records into functions accepting structural records is a big difference between today's opaque types and the proposed custom types

with the comments earlier in this thread this is only true if it's within the same module as the custom type is defined, right? Otherwise outside the module, you must annotate the structural type as the custom type, and likewise for the function being called in the defining module. sorry if i'm not getting it right
I believe the original idea was that if you create a custom record by default none of its fields are visible but you can chose to expose them

My thought is that you would be able to pass a custom record into a function accepting a structural record as long as the fields are visible in that module, either because it is the module where the custom record is defined, or because the fields are exposed and imported

Sam Mohr said:

I think the original discussion was that we wanted to make a simple way to define concrete types that can be passed over FFI worry-free

yeah, the motivations are:

  • we no longer support recursive structural types, and opaque types shouldn't be allowed to cross the host boundary. Putting the two together, it is currently impossible to send recursive types to the host, which is a limitation I would like to remove.
  • There are a number of features custom tag unions in particular would unlock, such as Bool being able to be just True and False again (while still having custom serialization logic, which was not possible back when it was a structural type), Result being able to have methods run on it in the static dispatch world (e.g. my_result.map_err(...) working), being able to customize numbers/strings associated with different tags in a custom union (e.g. error codes), being able to mark tag unions as non-exhaustive so they can be added to without breaking backwards compatibility, etc.
  • Nominal tuples would allow for matrix types whose fields can be accessed using .0, .1, etc. while also being able to work with operator overloading and other customizations
  • Opaque wrappers work fine as a way to hide implementation details, but they seem to be harder to understand than e.g. Rust's public/private fields on structs
I think the public/private fields in custom records may not be a feasible idea after all, based on the above discussion
obviously having the option of public/private tags in custom tag unions is an option that would work, since that's been used by plenty of languages (Haskell, Elm, etc.)
the main downside there is that it's a bit verbose to wrap things in a single-tag union and then destructure that, e.g. my_fn = |Email.Email(str)| or something like that
another downside, which I do consider fairly minor, is that we'd end up with only having the concept of public/private for custom tag unions and nothing else in the language

which feels weirder to me than saying "everything is always public whether you're nominal or structural, and then we have this separate language feature for wrapping arbitrary types in a private wrapper"
i don't think disallowing recursive structural types can work unless it's also mandatory that nominal types are explicitly marked as custom types

i.e.

walk = |l| when l is Nil -> Nil; Cons x -> Cons (walk x)

has a type annotation or the constructors are wrapped with a nominal name
requiring type annotations would mean that not everything can be inferred

Richard Feldman said:

the main downside there is that it's a bit verbose to wrap things in a single-tag union and then destructure that, e.g. my_fn = |Email.Email(str)| or something like that

I don't think this is too bad if you expose a getter. If you don't expose a getter it's probably not used very often.

Ayaz Hafiz said:

i.e.

walk = |l| when l is Nil -> Nil; Cons x -> Cons (walk x)

has a type annotation or the constructors are wrapped with a nominal name

yep, in the design doc they're Rust-style, but with dots - e.g . you'd writeResult.Ok(val) (but then you can also import them unqualified, which would be the case with Result and Bool by default so you can just say Ok and True)

Ayaz Hafiz said:

Richard Feldman said:

the main downside there is that it's a bit verbose to wrap things in a single-tag union and then destructure that, e.g. my_fn = |Email.Email(str)| or something like that

I don't think this is too bad if you expose a getter. If you don't expose a getter it's probably not used very often.

yeah, it's not the end of the world or anything haha...I've done it for years with Elm, just always felt like it would have been nice to try to make it more concise

if custom types are automatically inferred from structural types, that automatically breaks principal inference for recursive types, no? so either there needs to be a carve out for inference working only in special cases, or it might be better to require wrapping all custom types in a constructor
https://docs.google.com/document/d/10OFeNl9KAYAErajE0Wio4AAR66yM2u13bku0mTUawVk/edit?tab=t.0 is the doc - that's the idea
when creating one, you have to say e.g. MyRecord.{ x: 1, y: 2 }
instead of { x: 1, y: 2 }

I think the public/private fields in custom records may not be a feasible idea after all, based on the above discussion

I think they could work with what I proposed. What do you think of that idea?
however, if foo.x and foo.yare able to work on both custom types and structural types, then that implies that { x, y } = foo would work too
for accessing them, not creating them
and if foo.x and foo.y can't work on custom records, the ergonomics would be so bad it probably wouldn't make sense to support custom records at all (and just allow custom tag unions)
foo.x can work on custom records with your design if you just expose a getter right

my understanding is this is the only problematic part

…but now you can also choose to implement that last one as:

toStr : Email -> Str
toStr = \email -> email.0

to me that seems like a really minor inconvenience. the author of the custom type will provide the access patterns the custom type should be using. if there are unknown access types it's probably pure data and maybe better as a structural type. just my 2c
I think that one is equivalent to record field access
(I gotta step out for a few, @Isaac Van Doren I'll comment on your idea when I get back!)

Isaac Van Doren said:

I think if you want a function argument type to be a custom type, you should have to explicitly specify it in the function declaration.

# The type stays the same with or without the annotation
double_left : U64Pair -> U64
double_left = |U64Pair.item| # insert actual syntax here
    item.left * 2

[...]
This maintains the property that adding or removing a type annotation won't change if your code compiles and it doesn't require the addition of a notion of opaque types in addition to custom types.

so I agree that this is technically true, but it seems to me that this is essentially a mandatory type annotation that's being treated as something other than a type annotation for the purpose of technically maintaining the property that type annotations are never mandatory :sweat_smile:

in the sense that if I put this into the repl:

|U64Pair.item| item

...the repl will tell me that the type of this function is:

U64Pair -> U64Pair
which means that the U64Pair. prefix is not actually doing anything other than confirming what the type of item is, which is 100% exactly what a type annotation's job is

in contrast, if I do either of these:

|@U64Pair(item)| item # today's "opaque wrapper" design

|U64Pair.U64Pair(item)| item # potential "private custom tags" design

...the inferred arg type and return type will be different, because these wrappers actually have meaning at the value level

this does remind me that one of the original motivations for opaque types was to get a nominal type (they weren't just for information hiding), and if we're having separate nominal types, that's an argument against continuing to have opaque types

Gleam basically only does the enum-only custom type approach, I'm pretty sure to match Erlang. It's obvious to us that Email.Email({ text: Str }) is duplicative because we know that there could be a Email({ text: Str }), but I don't think it's that bad if it's all we have.
Having structural, opaque, and custom types would be pretty strongly against a sense of "one way to do things"
If there's any way to pick either nominal types OR opaque types, we should pick only one even if it's slightly more awkward

so I agree that this is technically true, but it seems to me that this is essentially a mandatory type annotation that's being treated as something other than a type annotation for the purpose of technically maintaining the property that type annotations are never mandatory :sweat_smile:

Right for sure, the key thing for me is that only changing the custom type name/annotation thing could meaningfully effect your program while type annotations otherwise are completely optional. That seems fine and desirable
What if you have a custom value inside of some other container, like a list?

Say,

Address := { name: Str, other_fields: _ }

use_addresses = |addresses| ->
    Address.first_address = addresses.first()?

I worry that exposing getters for every field in a record will become very common in a static dispatch world if we only have opaque types or nominal types where fields can't be exposed because there will be so much more of a reason to use nominal types. That sounds disturbingly java-esque to me :sweat_smile:

What if you have a custom value inside of some other container, like a list?

Can you elaborate?
Oh, no, nevermind

I was pointing out that you can't annotate a function with your prefix idea if it acts on a collection of the module's custom type, but that function operating on List Address wouldn't be a method for Address

Could we allow destructuring of public custom records in other modules if they're imported in some explicitly public way?

-- Address.roc
module [Address.*, gen to_str]

Address := { street: Str, city: Str }

-- Calculate.roc
module [calc]

import Address exposing [Address.*]

calc = |addresses|
    addresses.map(|Address.{ street, city }| street)

    ...
Seems a bit more awkward, but unlocks the capacity to have public custom records

random thought: we could make it more concise by saying:

|U64Pair.(item)| item

...is syntax sugar for:

|U64Pair.U64Pair(item)| item

the idea being that the first example only works on a custom single-tag union, and it's just syntax sugar that means you don't have to write out the whole tag name since there's only one
I think that would totally work, assuming we can't come up with a way to make this work otherwise
I think that has essentially the same conciseness as Isaac's idea
just formulated as syntax sugar

(I don't think we should have |U64Pair.item| be the syntax in either case, because I'd still like to use that syntax in patterns for matching on named constants - like Bool.true -> even though I know that doesn't actually work today)
How much more annoying will FFI be for single tag unions with records in them compared to just records?
Maybe we can have single tag unions automatically unwrap to their inner data when being passed to/from the host?
Probably would need to be some opt-in behavior, since it's a little weird to do it based on the tag amount

Sam Mohr said:

Maybe we can have single tag unions automatically unwrap to their inner data when being passed to/from the host?

we already do this in general
same with single-field records
there's actually no possible way to send either of them to the host :big_smile:
I know we do that for the runtime repr
Okay, great
Oh, read that wrong
the wrapper just has no runtime representation in memory, which is all the host cares about
it just cares about what bytes are coming across, and the wrappers are 0 bytes
so the host sees exactly the same bytes as the inner data
I guess you could define a single-field struct in the host and it wouldn't do anything
right
Yeah, makes sense
In the future, are we planning on having any kind of validation of the interface between the host and the platform?
ideally that's what glue does
We'd need to be conscious of this there, but that much should be obvious once we get to that point
Assuming you use glue, yes
well it's not really possible to validate it without knowing the host language

like if we gave Roc an understanding of Rust, then we could theoretically parse the Rust source code and see if the FFI declarations line up with the Roc types, but glue does a more helpeful thing by just generating those FFI declarations in the first place :big_smile:
Makes sense. I assumed there was probably some black magic way to read an object file and check the function defs or something
But nope
yeah once they cross the boundary they're just a bunch of ones and zeros, no metadata in memory :big_smile:
Well, this makes implementation easy!

the other benefit of explicitly requiring the constructor in all pattern matches and value creation is it makes reading code much easier. no need to try to figure out if something is just data or a nominal type.
totally
I think that part of the design is fine, no concerns with it
and also seems fine to have custom records and tuples with all-public fields
really I think the only question is how hiding should work
and right now it's feeling to me like giving custom tag unions control over whether to expose their tags is the frontrunner (like how Elm and Haskell do it, which would replace today's opaque types)

Tangential question: how would non-exhaustive tags work? If you can either make the tags of a union all public or all private, then if they're all public, you can iterate over all of them, and if they're all private, you can't access them anyway. Unless there's a plan to allow exporting specific tags in a custom union?
the idea is that if you're pattern matching on them, you must include a default branch no matter what (so, _ ->)
I can't think of a reason this would be useful if you're keeping the implementation private

the reason to do it in a public tagged union is to convey "I plan to introduce new tags to this in the future, and I want those to be nonbreaking changes for you" - e.g. a tag union of file I/O errors that come from the OS, and sometimes the OS introduces new ones

arguably it's better to only give people the option to make a breaking change like that, so everyone who chose to exhaustively match on it gets an exhaustiveness error when they upgrade and can decide how to handle it

the argument against that is that if you have a foundational package that's depended on by tons of other packages (which a package involving file I/O errors absolutely could be), then making a breaking release could really fracture the ecosystem

which in turn would create a situation where maybe the package author just chooses not to add support for new OS file I/O errors because the breaking release would be too disruptive, so they just get out of sync with the OS APIs - either that or fracture the ecosystem

so having the option to declare it as non-exhaustive means that in the future you can add as many new tags as you like, and they'll never break anyone because everyone already has a _ -> branch covering unanticipated future tags
So we want people to either use Result methods or inexhaustively match on Ok, Err, and _?
It's a minor cost, but I don't think we'd want to block exhaustiveness checking for Results
Not ideal, but we could special case Bool and Result
I agree that if we have to pick between "all public tag unions are exhaustive" or "all of them are inexhaustive", inexhaustive is better
We could also just have _Private tags
Python-style
Meaning that custom tag union can uniquely have variants with private tags, denoted by an underscore in the start of the name
And any of those variants can't be publicized
oh it would be an optional thing
rarely used
like maybe you could add .. to your custom tag union definition to say "make this custom tag union be non-exhaustive"
I've been DEEP in the PNC fuzzer fix, but I wanted to register that I am down with custom types (and hopefully that will be our only nominal-ish type) being tag union only
It at least gives a single way to do things, and the only change needed to support it is to make the constructors be namespaced and explicitly exposed.
From a syntactic level
Oh, and getting rid of the opaque ref syntax
But if we have this AND another nominal typing mechanism for plain records - I would be opposed
The .. reuse works for me, it's close enough to the normal meaning of .. in tag unions to not be a weird, double use of the syntax, and it allows the author to decide whether to use it. I'm in!
